ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for fried beancurd shrimp roll; mapped to closest parent class 'fried food product' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tofu and tofu products — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a beancurd-skin-wrapped fried seafood roll; shrimp component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fried beancurd shrimp roll. A0CKP (tofu and tofu products) is used as the primary matrix anchor. Shrimp filling is a secondary component not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried beancurd shrimp roll as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not systematically cover composite Asian prepared foods at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one fried beancurd shrimp roll weighing approximately 70 g, commonly served as a side dish or snack at hawker centres and zi char stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Beancurd Shrimp Roll — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Beancurd Shrimp Roll (F03060009). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed protein-fat-fibre dish with low net carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried beancurd shrimp roll. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous mixed protein-fat-soy dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is dominated by protein (16 g/100g), fat (19 g/100g), and dietary fibre (2.4 g/100g), with only 7.6 g/100g carbohydrate and 2.2 g/100g sugar, strongly attenuating glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 70 g serving = (40 × 7.6 × 0.70) / 100 ≈ 2.1 g, classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 459 mg/100g with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: At 459 mg sodium per 100 g, a single 70 g serving contributes approximately 321 mg sodium — roughly 14% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — and individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid high-sodium dipping sauces, and request reduced soy sauce in preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate and high protein support glycaemic stability, but deep-frying adds excess fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 7.6 g carbohydrate and 2.2 g sugar per 100 g, this dish has a low glycaemic impact; however, the high fat content from deep-frying (19 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance over time, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit frequency and prefer steamed or baked alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat from deep-frying and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of 19 g total fat per 100 g (including approximately 3.5 g saturated fat) and 459 mg sodium raises cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally, in small portions, and choose steamed or baked preparations to reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trace trans fat, both pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation and trace trans fat from high-temperature frying; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish and request a steamed or baked version to retain the anti-inflammatory benefit of shrimp omega-3 and beancurd protein.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Beancurd Shrimp Roll (炸豆腐虾卷) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean hawker and zi char dish consisting of seasoned shrimp filling wrapped in dried beancurd skin (tofu skin, also known as yuba) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is thin, crunchy, and slightly blistered from the hot oil, while the interior is moist, savoury, and dense with shrimp and beancurd protein. The dish is commonly served as a side dish, appetiser, or snack at hawker centres, zi char stalls, and dim sum restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 265 kcal, 16 g protein, 19 g fat, and only 7.6 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Dietary fibre is 2.4 g/100g and sodium is elevated at 459 mg/100g due to seasoning and soy-based marinades. The glycaemic index is estimated as low (approximately 40) given the dominant protein, fat, and fibre matrix. Clinically, the dish is borderline for hypertension due to sodium content, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to deep-frying fat load, and unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation from deep-frying. It contains soy and crustacean allergens. A typical serving is approximately 70 g (one roll).

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03060009) at confidence 0.95, which is a primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI component only) but is clearly documented. Overall record confidence is high given exact HPB source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific fried beancurd shrimp roll preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03060009 as of 2026. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central analogous fried tofu and shrimp composite data, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable mixed protein-fat-soy dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. The input record listed dietary_tags including 'plant_based' which has been removed as the dish contains shrimp and is not plant-based. The input glycemic_index of 0 with confidence 0.346 from 'Embedded GI Database' has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database per Veritas source policy.
